bibliographic_data.title: Crowdsourcing for Identification of Polyp-Free Segments in Virtual Colonoscopy Videos
bibliographic_data.abstract: Virtual colonoscopy (VC) allows a physician to virtually navigate within a reconstructed 3D colon model searching for colorectal polyps. Though VC is widely recognized as a highly sensitive and specific test for identifying polyps, one limitation is the reading time, which can take over 30 minutes per patient. Large amounts of the colon are often devoid of polyps, and a way of identifying these polyp-free segments could be of valuable use in reducing the required reading time for the interrogating radiologist. To this end, we have tested the ability of the collective crowd intelligence of non-expert workers to identify polyp candidates and polyp-free regions. We presented twenty short videos flying through a segment of a virtual colon to each worker, and the crowd was asked to determine whether or not a possible polyp was observed within that video segment. We evaluated our framework on Amazon Mechanical Turk and found that the crowd was able to achieve a sensitivity of 80.0% and specificity of 86.5% in identifying video segments which contained a clinically proven polyp. Since each polyp appeared in multiple consecutive segments, all polyps were in fact identified. Using the crowd results as a first pass, 80% of the video segments could in theory be skipped by the radiologist, equating to a significant time savings and enabling more VC examinations to be performed.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study demonstrates the use of crowdsourcing to identify polyp-free segments in virtual colonoscopy videos, reducing reading time for radiologists and enabling more VC examinations.
core_content.key_methodology: Non-expert workers were presented with short video segments from a virtual colonoscopy fly-through and asked to determine if a possible polyp was observed. The crowd achieved 80% sensitivity and 86.5% specificity in identifying polyp-free regions.
outcomes: The crowd could identify polyp-free regions with high accuracy.
Using the crowd results, radiologists could skip 80% of video segments during initial screening.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Virtual colonoscopy (VC) is a widely recognized method for identifying colorectal polyps. However, it requires significant reading time by radiologists, which can be reduced using crowdsourcing techniques. This study tested the ability of non-expert workers to identify polyp-free regions in virtual colonoscopy videos. Each worker was shown short video segments and asked if a possible polyp was observed. The crowd achieved 80% sensitivity and 86.5% specificity, allowing radiologists to skip 80% of video segments during initial screening.
